elements.sent-2411-001: Benzophenone serves as a prototype chromophore for studying the photochemistry of aromatic ketones, with applications ranging from biochemistry to organic light-emitting diodes.
elements.sent-2411-002: In particular, its intersystem crossing from the first singlet excited state to triplet states has been extensively studied, but experimental or theoretical studies on the preceding internal conversion within the singlet manifold are very rare.
elements.sent-2411-003: This relaxation mechanism is particularly important because direct population transfer of the first singlet excited state from the ground state is inefficient due to its low oscillator strength.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this work, we aim to fill this gap by employing mixed quantum classical and full quantum dynamics simulations and time-resolved photoelectron spectroscopy for gas-phase benzophenone and meta-methyl benzophenone.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our results show that nonadiabatic relaxation via conical intersections leads to a linear increase in the population of the first singlet excited state.
elements.sent-2411-006: This population transfer due to conical intersections can be directly detected by a bifurcation of the photoelectron signal.
elements.sent-2411-007: In addition, we are able to clarify the role of the third singlet excited state degenerate to the second excited state --- a topic that remains largely unexplored in the existing literature on benzophenone.
elements.sent-2411-008: This type of chromophore has a rich photochemistry under \gls{uv light, which involves several nonadiabatic steps: a \gls{coin between the first two singlet excited states, $S_2$ and $S_1$, and subsequent efficient intersystem crossing processes between the $S_1$ and several triplet states.}}
elements.sent-2411-009: Especially due to the latter \gls{bp and many of its derivatives have significant applications in technological and biological contexts, including organic light-emitting diodes~\cite{Lee2014-iq,Jhulki2016-hi , \gls{uv filters~\cite{Li2016-km, Wong2021-gd , and photosensitizers~\cite{Cuquerella2012-be,Dumont2015-mp .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: The existing literature on the photochemistry of \gls{bp is extensive, but most experimental and theoretical studies~\cite{Aloise2008-pl,Spighi2014-hv,Sergentu2014-da,Favero2016-ht,Marazzi2016-lc,Shizu2021-ij focus on the mechanisms leading to direct and indirect population of the lowest triplet excited state, $T_1$, starting from $S_1$~\cite{Marazzi2016-lc .}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: However, the $S_1$ state has a negligible cross-section and cannot be efficiently populated directly from the ground state.
elements.sent-2411-012: The experimental more realistic pathway is to excite the system to higher lying singlet states of $\pi \pi ^*$ character, which have a stronger oscillator strength, and populate $S_1$ via internal conversion.
elements.sent-2411-013: The $S_2$/$S_1$ \gls{coin has been the subject of two experimental investigations by Shah et al.~\cite{Shah2004-zh and Spighi et al.~\cite{Spighi2014-hv .}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: In their study, Shah et al. employed time-resolved absorption spectroscopy with a \gls{fwhm $\approx150$~fs pump pulse centered at 267~nm ($\approx4.66$~eV).}
elements.sent-2411-015: They measured a lifetime of 0.53~ps for the $S_2$ state in acetonitrile following the growth in absorption of the $S_1$ state, and a kinetic rate of $1.7-1.9 \cdot 10^{12}$~s$^{-1}$ for the $S_2 \rightarrow S_1 $ internal conversion.
elements.sent-2411-016: Spighi et al. investigated the excited state dynamics of gas-phase \gls{bp via femtosecond and nanosecond resonance-enhanced multiphoton ionization, employing a pump pulse centered at 266~nm ($\approx4.66$~eV), with a pump-probe cross-correlation of approximately 100~fs.}
elements.sent-2411-017: The authors observed an exponential rise and decay of photoelectron bands with a time constant of 150~fs, which they attributed to internal conversion to $S_1$.
elements.sent-2411-018: However, as the authors stated, the multiphoton ionization probe made it impossible to unambiguously assign each band in the photoelectron spectra to a single electronic state.
elements.sent-2411-019: The resonance-enhanced multiphoton ionization scheme involves several intermediate states providing multiple pathways and resonance conditions, further complicating the interpretation.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.4\linewidth]{molecular_diagram.png} \caption{Valence molecular orbital diagram of ground-state \gls{bp} at the CAS(16,15)/ANO-L-VDPZ level of theory. The molecular orbitals are labeled according to $C_2$ symmetry. The numbering for the oxygen lone pair in the diagram is arbitrarily set to 1. An isovalue of 0.05 was used to generate the molecular orbital plots.} \label{BP_MO_diagram}
elements.sent-2411-020: Both experiments provide evidence of the ultrafast nonradiative decay from the $S_2$ state, indicating the presence of a conical intersection between $S_2$ and $S_1$.
elements.sent-2411-021: However, the temporal resolution of the two experiments cannot resolve the intricate ultrafast excited-state dynamics occurring within the first 100~fs.
elements.sent-2411-022: Moreover, the existence of the $S_3$ state, which is almost degenerate to the $S_2$ state, has not been taken into account in the interpretation of the experimental results.
elements.sent-2411-023: The $S_1$ state has $n\pi^*$ character and belongs to the irreducible representation $A$ under $C_2$ symmetry.
elements.sent-2411-024: The $S_0 \rightarrow S_1$ transition predominantly involves the promotion of an electron from the oxygen lone pair, $1b$, to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), $4b$ (see Fig.~\ref{BP_MO_diagram ).}
elements.sent-2411-025: Both $S_2$ and $S_3$ have $\pi \pi^* $ character and belong to the irreducible representations $A$ and $B$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-026: The $S_0 \rightarrow S_2$ transition primarily involves $\pi$ electron excitations from $2b$ to $3b$ and from $2a$ to $3a$; while the $S_0 \rightarrow S_3$ transition involves $\pi$ excitations from $1a$ to $4b$ and from $2b$ to $4a$.
elements.sent-2411-027: Given that the energy difference between $S_2$ and $S_3$ is predicted on the order of $10^{-2}$~eV, the experimental pump pulse resolution cannot differentiate them.
elements.sent-2411-028: Consequently, both states can be expected to be populated under experimental conditions~\cite{Shah2004-zh,Spighi2014-hv , adding another piece to the puzzle.}
elements.sent-2411-029: In this work we determine time-resolved photoelectron spectra based on the excited state dynamics in the singlet manifold of the gas phase \gls{bp and its methylated derivative \gls{mbp , displayed in Fig.~\ref{BP_mBP_mol_struct , to show that (i) the rise in the population of the $S_1$ state is linear, and it is accompanied by a similar linear increase in the corresponding photoelectron band.}}}
elements.sent-2411-030: Beyond that, the internal conversion can be associated with a bifurcation of the photoelectron signal; (ii) the inclusion of the $S_3$ state reveals ultrafast population transfer to $S_2$, due to the presence of a conical intersection in the vicinity of the \gls{fc point.}
elements.sent-2411-031: All calculations were performed in a reproducible environment using the Nix package manager together with NixOS-QChem~\cite{Kowalewski2022-zt (commit 9b40085ca).}
elements.sent-2411-032: Geometry optimizations and frequency analysis were carried out at the $\omega$B97X-D2~\cite{Chai2008-rw,Grimme2006-pj /6-311G(d)~\cite{Krishnan1980-fy level of theory using the Gaussian 16 RevC.01~\cite{Frisch2016-rb quantum chemistry program.}}}
elements.sent-2411-033: The excited states of \gls{bp and \gls{mbp were determined by \gls{lrdft , \gls{casscf as well as \gls{dftmrci theories.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-034: The \gls{lrdft calculations were carried out within the \gls{tda ~\cite{Hirata1999-ub using $\omega$B97X-D4~\cite{Najibi2020-fe /6-311G(d) level of theory in the ORCA 5.0.4 program package~\cite{Neese2012-bk,Neese2022-ra .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-035: The state-average SA-\gls{casscf calculations were performed using OpenMolcas v.24.02~\cite{F_Aquilante2020-ds,Li-Manni2023-ro , with the ANO-L-VDZP~\cite{Widmark1990-xz basis set, averaging over 4 states.}}}
elements.sent-2411-036: After the \gls{casscf calculation, dynamic electron correlation was added using \gls{xmscaspt2 ~\cite{Shiozaki2011-zl,Granovsky2011-ar .}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: The \gls{ci expansion for \gls{casscf scales nearly exponentially with the number of active orbitals and electrons.}}
elements.sent-2411-038: The full $\pi$-system of BP requires an active space of 16 electrons in 15 orbitals.
elements.sent-2411-039: The full CAS(16,15) active space is shown in the supporting information Fig.~S1.
elements.sent-2411-040: This computation is quite time-consuming, and \gls{caspt2 becomes impractical for the purpose of calculating excited state dynamics.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Although this active space can still be used to benchmark some critical points, it is not suitable for constructing global potential energy surfaces.
elements.sent-2411-042: For this reason, we employed a \gls{dftmrci approach to achieve balance between computational costs and accuracy.}
elements.sent-2411-043: Two-dimensional potential energy surfaces, diabatic couplings and \glspl{tdm of the ground state and three singlet excited states of \gls{mbp were calculated at the \gls{dftmrci (2)~\cite{Grimme1999-qv,Neville2022-ht,Costain2024-fo level using the QTP17~\cite{Jin2018-hm xc-functional, the QE8 hamiltonian~\cite{Costain2024-fo and the 6-311G(d) basis set.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-044: The quasi-diabatic states and potentials were obtained by a procedure using quasi-degenerate perturbation theory~\cite{Neville2024-or as implemented in GRACI~\cite{Neville2021-fp .}}
elements.sent-2411-045: The active space used in the \gls{dftmrci calculations is shown in figs.~S3 and S4 of the \gls{si .}}
elements.sent-2411-046: The raw values (energies, couplings, and transition dipole moments) were then interpolated with polyharmonic splines~\cite{Kowalewski2016-ot onto a fine grid ($N_1=N_2=128$ points), for the wave packet dynamics.}
elements.sent-2411-047: The molecular dynamics of \gls{bp and \gls{mbp were simulated using the SHARC ab initio dynamics package version 3.0~\cite{Mai2018-bl,Mai2023-yu .}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: The necessary energies, gradients and non-adiabatic couplings were calculated on-the-fly at the \mbox{\gls{tda /$\omega$B97X-D4/6-311G(d) level of theory.}}
elements.sent-2411-049: In total, 1000 initial conditions for the dynamics simulations were generated based on a Wigner distribution computed from harmonic vibrational frequencies in the optimized ground state equilibrium geometry.
elements.sent-2411-050: The underlying frequency calculations were performed at $\omega$B97X-D2/6-311G(d) level of theory.
elements.sent-2411-051: Altogether, 160 initial geometries and velocities, 68 in state $S_2$ and 92 in state $S_3$ were chosen stochastically.
elements.sent-2411-052: The SHARC ab initio surface-hopping algorithm uses a fully diagonal electronic basis~\cite{Mai2013,Mai2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-053: The integration of the nuclear motion was done with the Velocity-Verlet algorithm with a maximal time of 0.5~ps using a time step of 0.5~fs.
elements.sent-2411-054: In each time step, gradients were computed for all states which are closer than 0.001~eV to the active state.
elements.sent-2411-055: The coefficients of the electronic wave function are propagated on interpolated intermediates with a time step of 0.02~fs applying a local diabatization technique~\cite{Granucci2007-ou in combination with the WFoverlap code~\cite{Plasser2016 to compute the wave function overlaps.}}
elements.sent-2411-056: Decoherence correction was taken into account using the energy-based method of Granucci and Persico with the parameter $\alpha$~=~0.1~a.u.~\cite{Granucci2010-cq We restricted our analysis to trajectories with a maximum change in the total energy of 0.3~eV and a maximum change in the total energy per step of 0.2~eV.}
elements.sent-2411-057: All trajectories are included in the analysis as long as both criteria are fulfilled.
elements.sent-2411-058: At 500 fs, 55{\% of the trajectories for \gls{bp and 50{\% of the trajectories for \gls{mbp met the energy conservation criteria.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-059: For each geometry, Dyson norms were calculated between 4 neutral and 4 cationic states through the WFoverlap code~\cite{Plasser2016,Ruckenbauer2016-ep at the \mbox{\gls{tda /$\omega$B97X-D4/6-311G(d) level of theory.}}}
elements.sent-2411-060: Excitation energies and corresponding Dyson norms were employed to generate a photoelectron spectrum, which was then broadened by a Gaussian function with a full width at half maximum of 0.4~eV.
elements.sent-2411-061: The wave packet dynamics of the photoexcited \gls{mbp was calculated by solving the time-dependent (non-relativistic) Schr\"odinger equation numerically with the Fourier method~\cite{Feit1982-mr,Kosloff1983-yl,Tannor2007-rl , using the in-house software package QDng~\cite{Kowalewski2024-va .}}}
elements.sent-2411-062: To propagate the wave packet on the potential energy surfaces, we used a Hamiltonian in a diabatic basis and coordinates $q$: \begin{equation}\label{eq:H} \hat{H} = \begin{pmatrix} \hat{T} + \hat{V}_{\mathrm{S_0}}(q) 0 0 0 0 \hat{T} + \hat{V}_{\mathrm{S_1}}(q) \hat{V}_{\mathrm{S_1}\mathrm{S_2}}(q) 0 0 \hat{V}_{\mathrm{S_1}\mathrm{S_2}}(q) \hat{T} + \hat{V}_{\mathrm{S_2}}(q) \hat{V}_{\mathrm{S_2}\mathrm{S_3}}(q) 0 0 \hat{V}_{\mathrm{S_2}\mathrm{S_3}}(q) \hat{T} + \hat{V}_{\mathrm{S_3}}(q) \end{pmatrix} \, ,\end{equation} where $\hat{T}$ is the kinetic energy operator, $\hat{V _{\mathrm{S_0 }$, $\hat{V _{\mathrm{S_1 }$, $\hat{V _{\mathrm{S_2 }$ and $\hat{V _{\mathrm{S_3 }$ are the diabatic potentials for the states $S_0$, $S_1$, $S_2$ and $S_3$, respectively.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-063: The diabatic couplings are given by $\hat{V _{\mathrm{S_1}\mathrm{S_2 }$ and $\hat{V _{\mathrm{S_2}\mathrm{S_3 }$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-064: The kinetic operator is in the G-matrix form~\cite{Stare2003-vi : \begin{equation}\label{T_Gmatrix} \hat{T} = -\dfrac{\hbar ^2}{2} \sum _{r=1} ^M \sum _{s=1} ^M \left[ G^{rs} \dfrac{\partial ^2}{\partial q_r \partial q_s} \right] \, ,\end{equation} where $M$ is the number of coordinates taken into considerations.}
elements.sent-2411-065: The expression in~\eqref{T_Gmatrix derives from the assumption that the elements of the Wilson G-matrix are constant.}
elements.sent-2411-066: The term $G^{rs}$ indicate the element of kinetic energy matrix equal to: \begin{equation}\label{G_matrix} G^{rs} = \sum _{i=1} ^{3N} \dfrac{1}{m_i} \dfrac{\partial q_r}{\partial x_i}\dfrac{\partial q_s}{\partial x_i} \, .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-067: Here $N$ is the total number of atoms and $m_i$ the mass of atom $i$.
elements.sent-2411-068: In Eq.~\eqref{G_matrix , diagonal entries are reciprocals of generalized reduced masses, and the off-diagonal entry is the kinetic coupling term.}
elements.sent-2411-069: The calculated values are given here in atomic units: $G_{rr = 0.0001447$, $G_{ss = 0.00016924$ and $G_{rs =-5.101169 \cdot 10^{-6}$~$\mathrm{au ^{-1}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-070: The short iterative Lanczos scheme~\cite{Lanczos1950-ex, Park1986-hj, Hochbruck2006-pa was used to propagate the nuclear wave packet and the Hamiltonian form Eq.~\ref{eq:H with a time step of $\Delta t = 1$~au (24.2~as).}}
elements.sent-2411-071: The total wave function was expressed as a linear combination \begin{equation}\Psi = \sum _{i=0} ^{4} c_i \phi _i\end{equation} with $c_0 = c_1 = 0$ , $c_2 =\sqrt{0.4}$ and $c_3 = \sqrt{0.6}$.
elements.sent-2411-072: These values were specifically chosen to replicate the initial populations of the surface hopping dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-073: Before we introduce the molecular dynamics and the simulated time-resolved photoelectron spectra for \gls{bp and \gls{mbp , we discuss the vertical UV excitations and ionization energies of the two molecules.}}
elements.sent-2411-074: Table~\ref{vert_energ_benchmark_BP provides a benchmark of calculated vertical excitations for \gls{bp against the experimental values taken from reference~\cite{Itoh1985-jk .}}}
elements.sent-2411-075: This includes the level of theory used in surface hopping dynamics (\gls{tda ), the level of theory used to construct \glspl{pes (\gls{dftmrci ), and the multireference method (CASPT2) that employs the full $\pi$ system of \gls{bp (CAS(16,15) with 4-state averaging).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-076: An even more extensive benchmark, including CASPT2 with different sizes of the active space and RASPT2 calculations, can be found in the \gls{si in tables~S1 to S4.}
elements.sent-2411-077: Additional computational details are given in the table caption and in section~\ref{sec:Comput_det .}
elements.figure-2411-003: \caption{Comparison between vertical excitation energies and their corresponding oscillator strength of \gls{bp}, calculated at \gls{tda}/$\omega$B97x-D4/6-311G*, \gls{dftmrci}(2)/QTP17/QE8/6-311G* and XMS-CASPT2/CAS(16,15)/ANO-L-VDPZ levels of theory.} \label{vert_energ_benchmark_BP} \begin{tabular}{lcccccccc} \multicolumn{4}{c}{Energies (eV)} \multicolumn{3}{c}{Oscillator Strength} TDA DFT/MRCI CASPT2 Exp TDA DFT/MRCI CASPT2 $S_{1}$ 4.04 4.04 3.65 3.61$^a$ 0.0010 0.0040 0.0007 $S_{2}$ 5.36 4.94 4.60 4.40$^a$ 0.015 0.0213 0.0036 $S_{3}$ 5.40 4.98 4.62 4.40$^a$ 0.015 0.0114 0.0023 \multicolumn{8}{l}{$^{a}$ low pressure vapor, ref.~\cite{Itoh1985-jk}} \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-078: As expected, the CASPT2 calculations predict vertical excitations very close to the experimental values and are therefore well suited as a general criterion for validating the other theoretical methods applied.
elements.sent-2411-079: In contrast, \gls{dftmrci and especially \gls{tda calculations generally overestimate the excitation energies.}}
elements.sent-2411-080: The \gls{dftmrci results are slightly more accurate, but still produce vertical transitions that are systematically blueshifted by approximately 0.4~eV.}
elements.sent-2411-081: Meanwhile, \gls{tda calculations tend to overestimate $\pi \pi ^*$ transitions by nearly 1 eV.}
elements.sent-2411-082: Nonetheless, all methods capture the near degeneracy between $S_2$ and $S_3$.
elements.sent-2411-083: Both \gls{dftmrci and \gls{tda predict an energy difference between $S_3$ and $S_2$ of 0.04~eV.}}
elements.sent-2411-084: According to CASPT2 calculations, the energy difference between $S_2$ and $S_1$ at the \gls{fc point is 0.95~eV, which is slightly above the experimental value of 0.79~eV.}
elements.sent-2411-085: Likely due to error compensation, the \gls{dftmrci method predicts a 0.9~eV gap, performing better than CASPT2.}
elements.sent-2411-086: The \gls{tda calculations yield the largest energy gap at 1.32~eV.}
elements.sent-2411-087: This overestimation of the $S_2$/$S_1$ energy gap may result in a slower population transfer in the surface hopping dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-088: All methods consistently predict the right trend for the oscillator strengths, identifying $S_0 \rightarrow S_1$ as the weakest transition.
elements.figure-2411-004: \caption{Comparison between vertical excitation energies and their corresponding oscillator strength of \gls{mbp}, calculated at \gls{tda}/$\omega$B97x-D4/6-311G* and \gls{dftmrci}(2)/QTP17/QE8/6-311G* levels of theory.} \label{vert_energ_benchmark_mBP} \begin{tabular}{lccccc} \multicolumn{2}{c}{Energies (eV)} \multicolumn{2}{c}{Oscillator Strength} TDA DFT/MRCI TDA DFT/MRCI $S_{1}$ 4.04 4.00 0.0010 0.0023 $S_{2}$ 5.25 4.80 0.0220 0.0279 $S_{3}$ 5.39 4.92 0.0133 0.0124 \end{tabular}

elements.sent-2411-090: Although we were unable to find any experimental UV spectrum data for gas-phase \gls{mbp to directly compare with our calculations, we anticipate that it will not differ significantly from that of the parent molecule.}

elements.sent-2411-092: The energy gap between $S_2$ and $S_1$ at the \gls{fc is calculated to be smaller for \gls{mbp than \gls{bp .}}}

elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width=0.5\linewidth]{Static_BZP_PS_tda_caspt2.png} \caption{Static photoelectron spectrum of ground-state \gls{bp} calculated with \gls{tda}/$\omega$B97X-D4 (red color scheme) and XMS-CASPT2 (blue color scheme). To prevent overlapping features, the CASPT2 spectrum is shown up-side-down.} \label{Static_BP_PS}
elements.subsection-2411-005: Semiclassical Dynamics and Time-resolved Photoelectron Spectra
elements.sent-2411-104: Having validated the ab initio method, we will now discuss the excited-state dynamics of \gls{bp and \gls{mbp , starting with the parent molecule.}}
elements.sent-2411-105: Note that the number of trajectories satisfying the total energy conservation criteria decreases with time.
elements.sent-2411-106: This time-dependent number of trajectories was used to construct the photoelectron spectra and the populations of the singlet manifold.
elements.sent-2411-107: The on-the-fly selection criteria are discussed in section~\ref{sec:Comput_det .}
elements.sent-2411-108: Upon excitation to the $S_2$ or $S_3$ state, for all analyzed trajectories nonadiabatic processes are observed.
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{on-the-fly_TDA_tr_PS_BZP_500fs.png} \caption{Time-resolved photoelectron spectrum of \gls{bp} computed at the \gls{tda}/$\omega$B97X-D4/6-311G* level of theory from the combined trajectories of $S_2$ and $S_3$. Upper panel: time evolution of the average adiabatic populations along the \gls{bp} trajectories. The second y-axis shows the fraction of trajectories (grey line) meeting the total energy conservation criteria at each time step, used for calculating the spectrum and time-dependent populations of the singlet manifold. A unitary fraction corresponds to 160 trajectories, with a minimum fraction of $\approx 0.55$ equivalent to 88 trajectories. Lower left panel: temporal slice of the time-resolved photoelectron spectrum selected at 0~fs. Dashed lines indicate binding energies at the Frank-Condon point, using the color scheme of the population plot. Lower right panel: full time-resolved photoelectron spectrum.} \label{BP_tr_PS_spectrum}
elements.sent-2411-109: The time evolution of the average adiabatic populations along the \gls{bp trajectories is shown in the top panel of Fig.~\ref{BP_tr_PS_spectrum .}}
elements.sent-2411-110: The second y-axis represents the fraction of trajectories (gray line) that satisfy the total energy conservation criteria at each time step.
elements.sent-2411-111: The trajectories reveal a rapid exponential decay of the $S_3$ population to $S_2$ within the initial 10~fs of the dynamics, and the nonadiabatic relaxation is completed within 50~fs.
elements.sent-2411-112: The strong depletion in the $S_3$ population suggests that $S_3$ and $S_2$ are strongly coupled and that there is a favorable gradient towards $S_2$.
elements.sent-2411-113: After 40~fs, an almost linear increase in the $S_1$ population can be observed, along with a corresponding decrease in the $S_2$ population.
elements.sent-2411-114: As more population goes into $S_1$ of $n\pi ^*$ anti-bonding character, an elongation of the CO bond can be observed, as shown in Fig.~S6 of the \gls{si .}
elements.sent-2411-115: The normal modes analysis of the SHARC trajectories within the first 150~fs reveals that the dihedral movement between the two aryl rings is predominant.
elements.sent-2411-116: Nevertheless, a direct correlation between the dihedral oscillations and the photoelectron observable could not be established.
elements.sent-2411-117: The normal mode analysis is available in the \gls{si in Fig.~S5.}
elements.sent-2411-118: The lower left panel of Fig.~\ref{BP_tr_PS_spectrum displays a snapshot of the time-resolved photoelectron spectrum taken at time zero.}
elements.sent-2411-119: The dashed lines indicate the binding energies at the \gls{fc point, with the same color scheme as the population plot in Fig.~\ref{BP_tr_PS_spectrum .}}
elements.sent-2411-120: The snapshot spectrum consists of two bands, approximately centered at 3.6 and 4.7~eV.
elements.sent-2411-121: With the temporal evolution of the populations in mind, we are now able to discuss the time-resolved photoelectron spectrum of \gls{bp , shown in the lower right panel of Fig.~\ref{BP_tr_PS_spectrum .}}
elements.sent-2411-122: Note that due to the different characteristic of the probe (multiphoton vs single photon ionization), we cannot directly compare our results to the experiment of Spighi et al.~\cite{Spighi2014-hv .}
elements.sent-2411-123: During the initial 15~fs of the dynamics, a distinct blueshift of the two bands is observed in the time-resolved photoelectron spectrum, along with a notable inversion in intensity.
elements.sent-2411-124: The band originating at 3.6~eV experiences a blueshift of approximately 0.4~eV and an increase in intensity, while the band at 4.7~eV experiences a blueshift of approximately 0.3~eV and a decrease in intensity.
elements.sent-2411-125: The internal conversion from $S_3$ to $S_2$ is responsible for this feature of the time-resolved photoelectron spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-126: The observed intensity exchange matches the exponential decay of the $S_3$ population.
elements.sent-2411-127: Moreover, the blueshift suggests a favorable gradient towards $S_2$.
elements.sent-2411-128: After 50~fs, the bifurcation of the band centered at 3.6~eV becomes increasingly more evident.
elements.sent-2411-129: The signal covers a broad spectral range, from 4 to 5.5~eV.
elements.sent-2411-130: Ultimately, two distinct bands can be identified after 190~fs.
elements.sent-2411-131: As will be shown later, this spectral feature is even more pronounced in the case of \gls{mbp .}
elements.sent-2411-132: In addition to the splitting of the signal, a distinctive change in intensity is also observed.
elements.sent-2411-133: In particular, the intensity of the new band emerging at 5.3~eV increases linearly, while the other decreases steadily.
elements.sent-2411-134: The nearly linear increase in the intensity profile of the new band is consistent with the time-dependent evolution of the $S_1$ population and its timescale.
elements.sent-2411-135: Therefore, we attribute the bifurcation in the photoelectron signal to the population transfer from $S_2$ to $S_1$.
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{m_BP_on-the-fly_TDA_tr_PS_BZP_500fs.png} \caption{Time-resolved photoelectron spectrum of \gls{mbp} computed at the \gls{tda}/$\omega$B97X-D4/6-311G* level of theory from the combined trajectories of $S_2$ and $S_3$. Upper panel: time evolution of the average adiabatic populations along the \gls{mbp} trajectories. The secondary y-axis shows the fraction of trajectories (grey line) meeting the total energy conservation criteria at each time step. A unitary fraction corresponds to 160 trajectories, with a minimum fraction of $\approx 0.5$ equivalent to 76 trajectories. Lower left panel: temporal slice of the time-resolved photoelectron spectrum selected at 0 fs. Dashed lines indicate binding energies at the \gls{fc} point, maintaining the color scheme of the population plot. Lower right panel: full time-resolved photoelectron spectrum.} \label{mBP_tr_PS_spectrum}
elements.sent-2411-136: The top panel of Fig.~\ref{mBP_tr_PS_spectrum shows the time evolution of the average adiabatic populations along the \gls{mbp trajectories.}}
elements.sent-2411-137: Although the observed features in the population dynamics are very similar to the parent molecule, some small differences also emerge.
elements.sent-2411-138: The depletion of the $S_3$ population remains exponential.
elements.sent-2411-139: However, a comparison between the two molecules reveals that, during the first 60~fs, the population transferred from $S_3$ to $S_2$ is about 10{\% greater in \gls{bp than in \gls{mbp .}}}
elements.sent-2411-140: After this initial phase, the populations in $S_3$ for both molecules become approximately the same.
elements.sent-2411-141: These features can be better appreciated in Fig.~S9 of the \gls{si .}
elements.sent-2411-142: These differences can be attributed to a larger energy gap between $S_3$ and $S_2$, resulting in weaker coupling of the states in \gls{mbp .}
elements.sent-2411-143: After a simulation time of 500~fs, 60{\% of the total population reside in $S_1$ for both molecules, we conclude that the presence of a methyl group in meta position does not significantly affect internal conversion from $S_2$ to $S_1$.}
elements.sent-2411-144: Similarly to the parent molecule, there is a direct correlation between the increase in population of $S_1$ and the elongation of the CO bond (Fig.~S6 in the \gls{si ).}
elements.sent-2411-145: The spectral snapshot of the time-resolved photoelectron spectrum at 0.0~fs, shown in the left panel of Fig.~\ref{mBP_tr_PS_spectrum , displays an intensity profile different from that of \gls{bp .}}
elements.sent-2411-146: In particular, the bands centered at 3.6 and 4.7~eV now show an equal intensity.
elements.sent-2411-147: The time-resolved photoelectron spectrum of \gls{mbp is displayed in Fig.~\ref{mBP_tr_PS_spectrum .}}
elements.sent-2411-148: As in the \gls{bp situation, the \gls{mbp spectrum exhibits a blueshift of $\approx 0.4$~eV of these two bands during the initial 15~fs of dynamics, paired with an exponential change in their intensity.}}
elements.sent-2411-149: Following this, the signal at 3.9~eV splits, resulting in a linear rise in the intensity of the $S_1$ signal, while the intensity of the $S_2$ signal decreases.
elements.sent-2411-150: Similarly to the parent molecule, the blueshift, accompanied by the exponential decay/rise in the intensity profile, mirrors the nonadiabatic relaxation from $S_3$ to $S_2$.
elements.sent-2411-151: The signal bifurcation and the linear increase in intensity of the newly emerged band correspond to the nonadiabatic relaxation from $S_2$ to $S_1$, and they appear more pronounced here than \gls{bp .}
elements.sent-2411-152: There is no clear connection between the dihedral oscillations of the aryl rings and the photoelectron spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-153: The construction of diabatic \glspl{pes for \gls{bp was unattainable due to numerical problems in the diabatization process, attributed to the degeneracy of $S_2$ and $S_3$, and the resulting coupling already at the \gls{fc point.}}}
elements.sent-2411-154: To address this issue, we opted for a methylated benzophenone as a candidate to construct diabatic \glspl{pes and carry out subsequent \gls{qd simulations.}}
elements.sent-2411-155: Indeed, despite the presence of a methyl group, the photochemistry of \gls{mbp does not change significantly, making the two systems quite comparable, as demonstrated by the \gls{uv spectrum based on a Wigner sampling in Fig.~\ref{wigner_spectrum .}}}
elements.sent-2411-156: Positioning the methyl group in meta yields a sufficiently large splitting between the $S_3$ and $S_2$ states enough to reduce their coupling at the \gls{fc point, without introducing a strong steric effect on the relaxation dynamics.}
elements.sent-2411-157: This, in turn, facilitates the construction of diabatic \glspl{pes .}
elements.sent-2411-158: Moreover, asymmetric substitution of the aryl rings breaks the $C_2$ symmetry of \gls{bp .}
elements.sent-2411-159: The number of vibrational \gls{dof for \gls{mbp is $3N-6=75$, where $N$ is the number of atoms in the molecule.}}
elements.sent-2411-160: In grid-based methods, the size of the nuclear wave function increases exponentially with the number of nuclear d.o.f., which makes the task computationally prohibitive to solve unless we restrict ourselves to the reactive subspace of the multidimensional problem.
elements.sent-2411-161: In such cases, a choice of reaction coordinate space is required.
elements.sent-2411-162: To describe the initial dynamics associated with the transfer of population from $S_2$ to $S_1$, the difference vector between the \gls{fc and \gls{coin geometries was selected as the primary coordinate: \begin{equation}\bm{v}_1 = \bm{R}^{\mathrm{FC}} - \bm{R}^{\mathrm{CoIn}} \, .\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-163: This ensures the presence of the $S_2$/$S_1$ \gls{coin in the coordinate space by construction.}
elements.sent-2411-164: The primary motion of this coordinate qualitatively describes a ring-puckering vibration.
elements.sent-2411-165: The analysis of the \gls{bp and \gls{mbp SHARC trajectories revealed that, within the first 150~fs, the dominant motion involved the dihedral angle between the two aryl rings.}}
elements.sent-2411-166: For this reason, the vector describing this motion was chosen as the second coordinate ($\bm{v _2$).}
elements.sent-2411-167: The two vectors appointed as reaction coordinates are depicted in \gls{si in Fig.~S7.}
elements.sent-2411-168: After removing the purely translational and rotational components from the two vectors by satisfying the Eckart conditions~\cite{Eckart1935-oy,Louck1976-ji , we orthonormalized the two coordinates using L{\"o wdin's orthogonalization scheme.}}
elements.sent-2411-169: We then calculated a 2D rectangular grid by scanning the two vectors using the \gls{fc point as reference geometry: \begin{equation}\bm{R}_{xyz} ^{new} = \bm{R}_{ref} + q_1 \bm{v}_1 + q_2 \bm{v}_2\end{equation} with coefficients $q_1$/$q_2$ in the range [-3, 3].}
elements.sent-2411-170: We conducted an unrelaxed scan of the reaction coordinates.
elements.sent-2411-171: This choice is supported by the timescale of the process we intend to describe, specifically the early sub-150-fs dynamics, during which we assume that additional relaxation does not play a dominant role.
elements.sent-2411-172: The diabatic \glspl{pes for \gls{mbp are displayed in Fig.~\ref{meta_BP_dPES .}}}
elements.figure-2411-009: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{meta_BZP_dPES_GRACI.png} \caption{2D diabatic potential energy surfaces of \gls{mbp}.The white dashed line indicates the diabatic intersection seam between $S_2$ and $S_1$ including the optimized \gls{coin}. A blue dashed line indicates the diabatic intersection seam between $S_3$ and $S_2$.} \label{meta_BP_dPES}
elements.sent-2411-173: The mixed quantum classical excited-state dynamics based on independent trajectories provides a clear picture of the nonadiabatic relaxation events in \gls{bp and \gls{mbp .}}
elements.sent-2411-174: The surface hopping methodology has the ability to tackle the full dimensionality of the nuclear problem; however, it cannot correctly describe coherent superpositions of electronic states.
elements.sent-2411-175: In fact, all dynamics start on a single adiabatic electronic state and not a superposition, such as one created by a laser pulse.
elements.sent-2411-176: Moreover, it does not properly account for decoherence in the nuclear subspace, caused by the nuclear motion.
elements.sent-2411-177: In contrast, an explicit full quantum dynamics simulation correctly describes coherent superpositions and the wave packet branching near a conical intersection, producing a short-lived electronic coherence.
elements.sent-2411-178: Information about the energy gap between the coupled electronic states is encoded in the oscillation period of these transient electronic coherences, and their detection can be used as a direct signature of \glspl{coin .}
elements.sent-2411-179: However, the grid-based approach we use is only possible for reduced geometric subspaces and not for the full 3$N$-6 degrees of freedom.
elements.sent-2411-180: For this reason, we have constructed global \glspl{pes in a reduced subspace of the 3$N$-6 nuclear \gls{dof of \gls{mbp , and used them for full \gls{qd simulations.}}}}
elements.figure-2411-010: \includegraphics[width=0.5\linewidth]{Sharc_QD_populations_coherences.png} \caption{Temporal evolution of diabatic populations and coherences in \gls{bp} and \gls{mbp} during the initial 120~fs. (a): diabatic populations as a function of time, derived from surface hopping (solid and dashed lines) and \gls{qd} (dash-dotted line) simulations. The populations of $S_2$ and $S_3$ are summed together; (b): magnitude of the electronic coherences between $S_2$ and $S_3$ as well as $S_1$ and $S_2$. Subscripts indicate the electronic states involved.} \label{QD_SHARC_pops_cohers}
elements.sent-2411-181: Figure~\ref{QD_SHARC_pops_cohers displays the temporal evolution of diabatic populations and electronic coherences, $\rho$, obtained from the \gls{qd simulations.}}
elements.sent-2411-182: The chosen reduced reaction coordinate space can capture the general trend for the $S_3$ to $S_2$ internal conversion, as shown in Fig.~S10 of the \gls{si .}
elements.sent-2411-183: However, the $S_3$/$S_2$ dynamics are likely washed out by laser pump-pulses longer than 15~fs.
elements.sent-2411-184: Moreover, the electronic coherence generated by the $S_3$/$S_2$ \gls{coin would be largely concealed by the one induced by the pump pulse.}
elements.sent-2411-185: Therefore, the populations of $S_3$ and $S_2$ are summed in Fig.~\ref{QD_SHARC_pops_cohers .}
elements.sent-2411-186: The \gls{qd simulations can qualitatively reproduce the population transfer from $S_2 + S_3$ to $S_1$ predicted by the surface hopping dynamics.}
elements.sent-2411-187: In particular, the nonadiabatic relaxation into $S_1$ begins at 40~fs and displays a similar linear growth.
elements.sent-2411-188: Despite this, there is a quantitative discrepancy in the total population transfer, as \gls{qd predicts a 0.05 $S_1$ population at 100~fs, while the surface hopping dynamics predicts 0.1.}
elements.sent-2411-189: A number of factors may account for this: the underlying electronic structure method used in the two approaches (TDA-LR-DFT vs DFT/MRCI) is different; the lack of electronic decoherence in semiclassical trajectory-based dynamics may influence the population transfer directly.
elements.sent-2411-190: The reaction coordinate space used for \gls{qd is two-dimensional and does not include all \gls{dof that the system may explore during the time interval of interest.}}
elements.sent-2411-191: Beyond 120~fs, these differences become more pronounced as the $S_1$ state is unable to relax within the chosen 2D reaction coordinate space.
elements.figure-2411-011: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Wave_packet_snapshots.png} \caption{Snapshots of wave packets (gold contour lines) at 0, 30, 60 and 80~fs. First row: wave packet evolution on $S_1$; second row: wave packet evolution on $S_2$; third row: wave packet evolution on $S_3$. The white dashed line indicates the diabatic intersection seam between $S_2$ and $S_1$ including the optimized \gls{coin}. A blue dashed line indicates the diabatic intersection seam between $S_3$ and $S_2$. } \label{wave_packet_snap}
elements.sent-2411-192: Figure~\ref{wave_packet_snap shows the time-dependent evolution of the nuclear wave packet on different \glspl{pes .}}
elements.sent-2411-193: Snapshots of the nuclear wave packet density ($\vert \Psi \vert ^2$) are captured at $t=0$, 30, 60 and 80~fs.
elements.sent-2411-194: Since any experimental pump pulse would populate both states, the \gls{qd simulations start in a coherent superposition of $S_2$ and $S_3$, thus generating a strong electronic coherence $\rho _{32}$ at 0~fs.}
elements.sent-2411-195: This coherence rapidly decays over 20~fs, after which it remains relatively constant around 0.1, with minor oscillations, as illustrated in Fig.~\ref{QD_SHARC_pops_cohers (b).}
elements.sent-2411-196: More interesting is the case of $\rho _{21}$ between $S_2$ and $S_1$.
elements.sent-2411-197: In fact, $\rho _{21}$ is zero for the first 35~fs of the dynamics and begins to increase as the wave packet approaches the \gls{coin , peaking around 70~fs.}
elements.sent-2411-198: The magnitude of $\rho_{21}$ is remarkably large and long-lived ($>50$~fs), especially considering that electronic coherences generated by \glspl{coin are typically on the order of $10 ^{-3}$ or less~\cite{Neville2022-og and decay very fast.}}
elements.sent-2411-199: Therefore, the calculated 2D \glspl{pes show promise for following studies where $\rho _{21}$ could be probed using time-resolved X-ray techniques~\cite{Schnappinger2022-pq , such as TRUECARS~\cite{Kowalewski2015-kv, Restaino2022-jw or time-resolved X-ray absorption spectroscopy.}}}
elements.sent-2411-200: Detection of $\rho _{21}$ could serve as a direct spectroscopic signature of the \acrlong{coin .}
elements.sent-2411-201: The excited-state dynamics of gas-phase benzophenone and its alkyl derivative, meta-methyl benzophenone have been investigated using mixed quantum classical and full quantum dynamics simulations, focusing on the nonadiabatic processes in the singlet manifold.
elements.sent-2411-202: The mixed quantum classical simulations were carried out at the \acrlong{tda \acrlong{lrdft level of theory using a surface hopping approach.}}
elements.sent-2411-203: The electronic structure method was benchmarked against experimental and theoretical data, assuring a qualitatively correct description of all states involved.
elements.sent-2411-204: We were able to analyze the excited state dynamics for \gls{bp and \gls{mbp for 500~fs, observing both the non-adiabatic relaxation process from $S_3$ to $S_2$ and then from $S_2$ to $S_1$.}}
elements.sent-2411-205: In addition to discussing the geometrical relaxation and the population dynamics, we also simulate an experimentally accessible observable, namely time-resolved photoelectron spectra for both molecules.
elements.sent-2411-206: In both cases, we observe an exponential decay of the population in the $S_3$.
elements.sent-2411-207: This behavior is observed in the time-resolved photoelectron spectra as a blueshift of 0.4~eV within the first 15~fs, immediately followed by an interchange in the intensity profile of the photoelectron bands.
elements.sent-2411-208: The population transfer to $S_1$ starts around 40~fs, and the increase/decrease in the population of $S_1$/$S_2$ is linear.
elements.sent-2411-209: As the system passes through the $S_2$/$S_1$ conical intersection, a bifurcation in the photoelectron signal appears in the spectra, followed by a linear increase in the $S_1$ band and a decrease in the intensity of the $S_2$ signal.
elements.sent-2411-210: To gain more insight and to correctly describe electronic coherences and superpositions of electronic states, we computed two-dimensional potential energy surfaces for meta-methylbenzophenone to perform full quantum dynamics simulations.
elements.sent-2411-211: The presence of the methyl group in the meta position increases the energy gap between the $S_3$ to $S_2$ states, which, however, only marginally affects the excited-state dynamics compared to \gls{bp .}
elements.sent-2411-212: This separation is in turn beneficial, as it leads to a slight decoupling of the two states at the Frank-Condon point, allowing for reasonable diabatization.
elements.sent-2411-213: The quantum dynamics simulations, carried out in a subspace of the full dimensional problem and in a diabatic basis, qualitatively reproduce the surface hopping dynamics over the first 120~fs of propagation.
elements.sent-2411-214: While the relaxation timescale and linear population increase are accurately depicted, surface hopping and quantum dynamics simulations differ quantitatively.
elements.sent-2411-215: The former predicts a population of 0.1 in $S_1$ at 100~fs, whereas the latter predicts 0.05.
elements.sent-2411-216: The discrepancy between the two approaches may be attributed to a number of factors, including the differing electronic structure theories employed, and the reduced reaction coordinate space utilized in quantum dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-217: The electronic coherence generated by the bifurcation of the nuclear wave packet at the $S_2$/$S_1$ conical intersection is remarkably large and survives for more than 50~fs.
elements.sent-2411-218: Consequently, the selected coordinate subspace appears promising for future investigations in which the electronic coherence might be probed with more advanced time-resolved X-ray techniques, which rely on electronic coherences.

elements.section-2411-008: Additional Benchmark of Vertical Excitation Energies
elements.sent-2411-226: Benzophenone in its ground state is a non-planar molecule with $C_2$ symmetry.
elements.sent-2411-227: The steric repulsion of hydrogen atoms causes rotation between the phenyl rings.
elements.sent-2411-228: We noticed that, starting from the actual twisted structure, the calculations produce incorrect $S_0$ and $S_1$ state energies.
elements.sent-2411-229: To correct for this, we first carried out a single-point CASSCF calculation on the planar structure and then used the resulting RasOrb file on the twisted ground-state structure.

elements.figure-2411-014: \includegraphics[width=0.7\linewidth]{active_space_16_15.png} \caption{Natural orbitals of benzophenone in the active space with 16 electrons in 15 orbitals, calculated at the CAS(16,16)/ANO-L-VDZP level of theory.} \label{active_space16_15}
elements.sent-2411-230: Sergentu et al.~\cite{Sergentu2014-da calculated a minimum energy path (MEP) at the CASPT2 level on a CAS(12,11) wave function with the ANO-L-VDZP basis set and stated that it does not lead to the $S_2$/$S_1$ conical intersection reporting an energy splitting of 0.69 eV.}
elements.sent-2411-231: They concluded that CAS(12,11) is inadequate to describe the state $S_2$ due to the absence of the lowest $\pi$ orbitals and corresponding $\pi ^*$ in the active space.
elements.sent-2411-232: We agree that an accurate characterization of the $S_2$ PES is crucial for understanding the photoexcitation dynamics, though our results differ slightly from their findings.
elements.sent-2411-233: In their paper, they did not specify which CASPT2 variant was used, but our replication of their results was successful only with multi-state (MS) CASPT2.
elements.sent-2411-234: We employed extended multi-state CASPT2 (XMS-CASPT2), which provides a better description of the states, particularly when state mixing is strong.
elements.sent-2411-235: Our benchmarks for gas-phase benzophenone vertical energies are in table~\ref{Table1 .}
elements.sent-2411-236: The analysis of the CI coefficients for $S_2$, $S_3$, and $S_4$ did not show significant contributions from the two lowest $\pi$ orbitals, suggesting that the improved description of $S_2$ is simply due to a larger active space, where the correlation of the $\pi$-system is better.
elements.sent-2411-237: This is evident in tables~\ref{Table1 and~\ref{Table3 .}}
elements.sent-2411-238: The $S_1$ state is unaffected by the active space size, but slightly influenced by the state-averaging number, with differences under 0.1 eV.
elements.sent-2411-239: The smaller active space (12,11) inadequately describes $S_2$, showing differences over 0.4 eV compared to the largest active space.
elements.sent-2411-240: So the conclusion reached by Sergentu et al. regarding this state still holds true.
elements.sent-2411-241: We have also benchmarked vertical excitations in the single manifold with RASPT2~\cite{Sauri2011-au,Malmqvist2008-ol .}
elements.sent-2411-242: The energies and the corresponding oscillator strength are reported in tables~\ref{Table3 .. and~\ref{Table4 .}}

elements.figure-2411-017: \includegraphics[width=0.7\linewidth]{active_space_ras.png} \caption{Natural orbitals of benzophenone in the restricted active space formalism with two orbitals in the RAS1, 11 orbitals in the RAS2, and two orbitals in the RAS3. 12 electrons in the RAS2, one hole and one particle allowed in the RAS1 and RAS3 respectively.} \label{active_space_ras}
elements.figure-2411-018: \includegraphics[width=0.7\linewidth]{active_space_graci_BP.png} \caption{Natural orbitals of benzophenone in the active space used in DFT/MRCI calculations. Two holes and two particles are allowed in the RAS1 and RAS3 respectively.} \label{DFT_MRCI_active_space_BP}
elements.figure-2411-019: \includegraphics[width=0.7\linewidth]{active_space_graci_mBP.png} \caption{Natural orbitals of meta-methyl benzophenone in the active space used in DFT/MRCI calculations. Two holes and two particles are allowed in the RAS1 and RAS3 respectively.} \label{DFT_MRCI_active_space_mBP}
elements.sent-2411-243: The activity of the normal modes of BP and m-BP for their respective ensemble of trajectories was calculated as implemented in SHARC~\cite{Kurtz2001-lg .}
elements.sent-2411-244: We restrict our analysis to the first 150 fs of the dynamics.
elements.figure-2411-020: \includegraphics[width=0.6\linewidth]{NMA_analysis_bar_plot.png} \caption{Normal mode analysis of the average trajectory for (a) benzophenone and (b) meta-methyl benzophenone, for the first 150 fs of the dynamics. Red oval indicate normal modes associated with the dihedral motion of the two aryl rings. Grey oval indicate normal modes associated with C-C stretching involving the carbon atom of the carbonyl group.} \label{NMA_analysis}
elements.figure-2411-021: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Geom_analysis_comparison.png} \caption{Mean progression of geometric parameters over time for BP (left column) and m-BP (right column). Top panel: fraction of trajectories (grey line) meeting the total energy conservation criteria at each time step; middle panel: mean temporal progression of the dihedral angle between the two aryl rings; bottom panel: mean temporal progression of the CO bond length. Red dashed lines indicate values at the FC point.} \label{}
elements.figure-2411-022: \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{PES_vectors.png} \caption{Graphical depiction of the orthonormalized vectors spanning the coordinate space used to form potential energy surfaces for m-BP. Vec1 represents the difference vector between the FC geometry and the CoIn geometry. Vec2 represents the dihedral angle between the aryl rings. } \label{meta_BZP_vecs_PES}
elements.figure-2411-023: \includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth]{meta_BZP_dPES_differences.png} \caption{Diabatic potential energy differences and diababtic couplings: (a) $S_2 - S_1$; (b) $S_3 - S_2$; (c) $S_2$/$S_1$ diabatic coupling; (d) $S_3$/$S_2$ diabatic coupling.} \label{meta_BP_PES_diff}
elements.section-2411-012: SHARC Populations of BP and m-BP
elements.figure-2411-024: \includegraphics[width=0.6\linewidth]{Sharc_pop_100fs_diff.png} \caption{Comparison of the adiabatic SHARC populations of the $S_2$ and $S_3$ states of benzophenone (solid lines) and meta-methyl benzophenone (dashed lines), within the initial 100~fs. } \label{sharc_pop_100fs}
elements.figure-2411-025: \includegraphics[width=0.65\linewidth]{Sharc_pop_diabtic_S2_S3.png} \caption{Temporal evolution of diabatic SHARC populations for the $S_2$ and $S_3$ states in benzophenone (solid lines) and meta-methyl benzophenone (dashed lines), alongside diabatic populations of meta-methyl benzophenone derived from QD simulations (dashed-dotted lines).} \label{fig:enter-label}
